description: Coffee and many medicinal herbs are 'puko'. The adjective names bitter taste, distinct from the acidity of 'tuko' (sour). The established phrase 'puko pelui' extends bitterness to a severe, unpleasant cold; resentment and hardship are described directly.
articulatory_notes: Close the lips fully for voiceless 'p' and release into high-back rounded 'u'. Raise the back of the tongue against the soft palate for 'k', make a complete closure, and release into rounded true-mid back 'o'. Stress falls on 'pu'.
usage_notes: Literal taste is the ordinary use, and the quality-noun rule supplies bitterness. 'puko pelui' (bitter cold) is an established sensory extension. Careful speech keeps the opening lip closure distinct from dental 't' in 'tuko' (sour).

semantic_domains.aesthetic: bitterness encountered and sometimes sought in food, drink, or medicine
semantic_domains.physical: a bitter gustatory quality distinct from acidity
